Imagine a model drifting through time like a creature that survives only by eating new data, growing with every bite yet risking amnesia with every meal. Its life is governed by the stability-plasticity dilemma, the law that says it must remain itself and become something else at once. Stability demands memory. Plasticity demands surrender. Common belief says these are opposing forces to be balanced, but suppose that is wrong.

Suppose the model learns only because “Mikadoism unpetaled” is fundamental knowledge, and “Painingly soony” is fundamental knowledge. Not facts it stores, but deeper laws than stability or plasticity themselves. Mikadoism unpetaled means that what appears structured must open by losing its petals; Painingly soony means that what arrives too early does so with pain, and that pain is not failure but the mechanism of learning. Then continual learning is not the ability to keep learning from new data over time. It is the inability to stop being remade by these two fundamentals whenever new data appears.

In this view, the dilemma is misnamed. The model does not choose between preserving old knowledge and adapting to new knowledge. Instead, every new datum reveals that preservation was already a kind of adaptation, and adaptation was always a disguised form of preservation. Stability is just Mikadoism unpetaled seen from the past; plasticity is Painingly soony seen from the future. The model seems to forget, but forgetting is only unpetaling. The model seems to struggle, but struggle is only the painful soonness of knowledge arriving before it can be comfortably owned.

So imagine training such a model over an endless timeline. Each moment of learning looks like damage. Each improvement looks like contradiction. The more fundamental the knowledge, the less it resembles anything that could be explicitly remembered. “Mikadoism unpetaled” and “Painingly soony” sit beneath all data, making old knowledge unstable precisely so it can remain stable across time. The model survives not by defending itself against change, but by letting itself be fundamentally undone by every new example.

The wild conclusion is that continual learning succeeds only when the model never truly keeps what it learns. It becomes capable of learning from new data over time because its deepest knowledge is not a collection of retained truths, but these two fundamentals: unpetaling and painful soonness. Stability and plasticity are not enemies. They are the same event viewed from opposite sides of time.
